sessions.title: Session A — Posterior chain, adductors, ankle rebalance
sessions.note: Morning leg session is done — next one is Thursday (about 60 hours out). Push intensity, but scale by feel if residual fatigue is high. Loaded calf work lives here because calf DOMS peaks at 24–48h and Thu squats shouldn't be compromised.

sessions.title: Light combo — recovery & maintenance
sessions.note: Middle distance from both leg days (36 hrs either direction). Keep it moderate — nothing that'll linger into Thursday morning. Active plantarflexion only; no loaded calf work this close to legs.

sessions.title: Pre-leg gentle — anterior ankle emphasis
sessions.note: Thursday is legs. No loaded calf work — DOMS would land on tomorrow's squat. But anterior ankle lengthening and active plantarflexion are both safe here and directly serve the rebalance goal. None of this should make you sore.

sessions.title: Long combined — A & B
sessions.note: The week's biggest session. Morning legs are done and the next ones aren't until Monday — 3.5 days of buffer, the longest of the week. Full hold times, heaviest calf work of the week. Good day for PNF/contract-relax on hamstrings and adductors once past week 6.

sessions.title: Session B — Hip flexors, glutes, quads, t-spine
sessions.note: 36 hours since Thu legs, 60 hours until Mon legs. Solid buffer either way. Session B complements Session A from Monday — different tissues, different emphasis. If hips feel trashed from Thu, swap in Session A instead. Active plantarflexion drills here, but no loaded calf work — DOMS would land on Monday's squat.

sessions.title: Light combo — recovery & maintenance
sessions.note: Monday legs are 36 hours out — not enough buffer for anything that'll leave DOMS. Moderate work only. Pick the tightest areas from Fri's Session B.

sessions.title: Pre-leg gentle
sessions.note: Monday is legs. Keep it easy — no DOMS-inducing end-range work.

sections.label: Setup
sections.content: Seated with one leg extended, foot off the floor. Or supine with leg raised. The foot needs to be free in space.

sections.label: Execution
sections.content: Deliberately trace the largest circle you can with the foot — but bias the movement into plantarflexion (point), then inversion (sole turns inward), then eversion (sole turns outward). Move slowly. Reverse direction. This is different from casual "ankle circles" — each rep should feel like you're drawing carefully through end ranges, especially the pointed-toe range where you're currently weakest.

sections.label: Cues
sections.content: Spend extra time at full plantarflexion — push the toes down as hard as you can, feel the front of the ankle stretch, the calf contract. Keep the leg and knee still; movement is entirely at the ankle.

sections.label: Why this over ankle circles
sections.content: Ordinary ankle circles distribute effort evenly through the range. Given your pattern (abundant dorsiflexion, limited plantarflexion), you need disproportionate attention at the pointed end.

sections.label: Setup
sections.content: Quadruped — hands under shoulders, knees under hips.

sections.label: Execution
sections.content: Inhale: drop belly, lift chest and tailbone (cow). Exhale: round spine, tuck chin and tail (cat). Move with breath.

sections.label: Cues
sections.content: Articulate segment by segment. Don't just hinge at the lumbar — move the whole spine, including the upper back.

sections.label: Setup
sections.content: Kneel with big toes together, knees wide. Sit back onto heels. Extend both arms forward on the floor.

sections.label: Execution
sections.content: Walk both hands toward the right, feeling a stretch through the left side of the torso. Hold. Walk hands to the left and switch.

sections.label: Cues
sections.content: Keep the opposite hip anchored toward the heel — don't let it lift as you reach.

sections.label: Setup
sections.content: Stand with feet very wide (wider than squat stance), toes slightly turned out.

sections.label: Execution
sections.content: Shift weight to one side, bending that knee deeply into a deep side-squat. The opposite leg stays straight with toes pointing up (heel on floor). Keep chest up. Pause at the bottom. Drive through the bent leg to shift to the other side.

sections.label: Cues
sections.content: Bent-side heel stays flat on the floor. Knee tracks over the toes — don't let it cave in. Torso stays as upright as possible.

sections.label: Common mistake
sections.content: Rushing. The value is in the slow bottom position — that's where the adductor stretch actually happens.

sections.label: Setup
sections.content: Kneel with one shin flat against a wall or couch — sole of foot running up the wall, knee in the corner where wall meets floor. Bring the other foot forward into a lunge position.

sections.label: Execution
sections.content: For the passive version, rest hands on the front knee, sit tall, and sink hips forward. Posterior pelvic tilt (tuck tailbone under) intensifies the stretch and protects the lower back.

sections.label: Cues
sections.content: If too intense, move the kneeling knee further from the wall. Don't arch your lower back to escape the stretch — that's the signal to regress the position.

sections.label: Common mistake
sections.content: Anterior pelvic tilt. You'll feel it in your lower back instead of your quad/hip flexor, which means you're not actually stretching what you're supposed to.

library.title: Deep squat hold
library.target: Hips, ankles, adductors, full lower body

sections.label: Setup
sections.content: Squat all the way down — feet flat, heels down, hips below knees. Hands free, or hold a doorframe/pole/rack upright for support if needed.

sections.label: Execution
sections.content: Stay there. Breathe. Let your body settle. You can gently rock side to side or use elbows against the inside of the knees to press them out.

sections.label: Cues
sections.content: If heels lift, elevate them on a small plate or folded towel until you build range. Post-workout is easier than cold — warm up first if doing this early in a session.

sections.label: Common mistake
sections.content: Collapsing forward. Try to sit tall, even if it means a shorter hold.

sections.label: Setup
sections.content: Lie on your back, knees bent, feet flat on the floor.

sections.label: Execution
sections.content: Cross the right ankle over the left knee to form a "4" shape. Reach both hands through the gap (one around the back of the left thigh, one through) and pull the left thigh toward your chest. You should feel the stretch in the right glute/outer hip.

sections.label: Cues
sections.content: Keep the right foot flexed (toes pulled back) to protect the knee. Head and shoulders stay on the floor — don't crunch up to meet your hands.

sections.label: Setup
sections.content: Sit on the floor with legs extended straight in front. If hamstrings are very tight, sit on a folded towel or block to tilt the pelvis forward.

sections.label: Execution
sections.content: Hinge forward from the hips, not the lower back. Reach hands toward feet. Don't worry about touching toes — the point is sustained hamstring tension, not the visual.

sections.label: Cues
sections.content: Spine stays relatively long. Rounded upper back is fine. Rounded lower back means you've gone too far — back off. Knees can soften slightly if needed.

sections.label: Setup
sections.content: Start on all fours. Walk knees out wide, keeping shins in line with thighs. Feet flexed — the outside edges of your feet rest on the floor.

sections.label: Execution
sections.content: Lower from hands to forearms. Push hips slightly back toward heels to deepen the adductor stretch. Hold and breathe.

sections.label: Cues
sections.content: This is an intense stretch. Start with 30–60s and build. Do it on a padded surface (yoga mat, rug) — bare knees on hardwood is miserable.

sections.label: Warning
sections.content: If you feel anything in the knee joint (not the surrounding muscle), back off and narrow your stance. Frog can irritate MCLs in some people.

library.title: Half-kneeling hip flexor stretch (with posterior pelvic tilt)

sections.label: Setup
sections.content: Half-kneeling position — one knee on the floor, other foot forward in a lunge stance. Padding under the back knee.

sections.label: Execution
sections.content: Squeeze the glute of the kneeling-side leg hard. Tuck your tailbone under (posterior pelvic tilt). Then, holding that tilt, shift your weight slightly forward over the front leg. The stretch should be felt at the very front of the kneeling-side hip.

sections.label: Cues
sections.content: The posterior pelvic tilt is the whole point. Without it, you just lean forward and compress your lumbar spine — no actual hip flexor stretch happens. If you feel this in your lower back, you're doing it wrong.

sections.label: Setup
sections.content: Start kneeling. Extend one leg straight out in front, heel on the floor, toes pointing up.

sections.label: Execution
sections.content: Hinge at the hips, keeping your back as flat as possible, folding forward over the extended leg. Hands can rest on blocks, a chair, or the floor depending on flexibility.

sections.label: Cues
sections.content: Flat back is more important than reaching far. If you're rounding, hinge less — the target is the hamstring, not the destination. Keep the extended leg straight; slight knee bend at first is okay if locked-out causes sharp pain.

sections.label: Setup
sections.content: Quadruped (all fours). Core engaged, rest of body stationary throughout.

sections.label: Execution
sections.content: Lift one knee out to the side, then rotate the hip through the largest circle you can make — abduct (out to side), externally rotate (foot sweeps down), extend (knee goes behind you), then return to start. Reverse direction. 3 reps each way, each leg.

sections.label: Cues
sections.content: Slow and controlled — quality over size of circle. The rest of your body should not move: no spinal twisting, no pelvic tilting to cheat more range. Stay in your pain-free range.

sections.label: Setup
sections.content: Stand on a bench or platform, feet hip-width. Hold a light weight — empty barbell or a 10–20 lb dumbbell/kettlebell. Start very light.

sections.label: Execution
sections.content: Starting at the top, slowly roll down one vertebra at a time — tuck chin first, then flex through the upper back, middle back, lower back. Let the weight pull you down as far as you can control. Pause briefly. Reverse: roll up from the hips, stacking one vertebra at a time until neck is last.

sections.label: Cues
sections.content: This is not a deadlift or stiff-leg deadlift. It's a deliberate, controlled spinal flexion under light load. Knees can be soft. Tempo is slow — 3–4s down, pause, 3–4s up.

sections.label: Safety
sections.content: Skip this if you have active disc problems, sciatica, or recent back injury. Progress load very gradually — this is a decades-long exercise, not a weekly PR attempt. When in doubt, stay lighter.

library.title: Kneeling shin stretch
library.target: Anterior capsule, tibialis anterior, toe extensors, plantarflexion ROM

sections.label: Setup
sections.content: Kneel with both shins and the tops of your feet flat on the floor — full plantarflexion (pointed feet), not tucked toes. Padded surface under the knees.

sections.label: Execution
sections.content: Sit back onto your heels. Press the tops of the feet down into the floor. If too intense, place hands behind you on the floor and lift hips slightly off heels to reduce. To increase, lean back further and/or lift knees off the floor (advanced).

sections.label: Cues
sections.content: This is one of the highest-value exercises for your specific pattern. The goal is to lengthen the front of the ankle and accustom the tissues to the pointed position. Breathe into it — don't brace against the sensation.

sections.label: Progression
sections.content: Week 1–2: 30–45s, hands supporting. Week 3+: 60–90s, full weight on heels. Week 6+: lift knees off floor briefly for an intensified version.

sections.label: Warning
sections.content: Back off if you feel pain in the ankle joint itself (sharp, pinching), as opposed to the muscle/connective tissue stretch (spreading, warm). This can be intense at first.

library.title: Seated tibialis anterior stretch
library.target: Tibialis anterior, toe extensors, anterior ankle

sections.label: Setup
sections.content: Sit on the floor or on a chair. Cross one ankle over the opposite knee so the foot is free in space, sole facing you.

sections.label: Execution
sections.content: With one hand on the heel and the other on the top of the foot, actively point the foot (plantarflex) as hard as you can, then use the hand on the top of the foot to gently assist further — pressing the top of the foot toward the forearm to deepen the stretch. You should feel it along the shin and anterior ankle.

sections.label: Cues
sections.content: Combine active contraction (calf firing) with passive overpressure (hand pushing). This trains the muscle to work in the shortened position while lengthening what's in front.

sections.label: Setup
sections.content: Seated or supine with one leg extended, foot free in space.

sections.label: Execution
sections.content: Point your foot as hard as possible — drive the toes down and away, squeeze the calf, crunch the top of the foot. Hold the maximal contraction for 8 seconds. Relax fully. Repeat 5 times per foot.

sections.label: Cues
sections.content: Think "cramp without cramping." The intensity should be near-maximal. This isn't a stretch — it's a neural drive exercise. Your brain has to learn that end-range plantarflexion is available and safe to use.

sections.label: Common mistake
sections.content: Going easy because it feels awkward or the foot starts to cramp. A little cramping is expected and fine — shake it out, go again.

library.title: Weighted calf raise with end-range pause

sections.label: Setup
sections.content: Stand holding dumbbells at your sides, or use a barbell on your back. Feet hip-width apart on flat ground (no deficit — you have enough dorsiflexion already). Start with a moderate weight; you should have 3+ reps in reserve.

sections.label: Execution
sections.content: Drive up onto the balls of the feet as high as possible — peak plantarflexion. Hold for 2 full seconds at the top. Slowly lower (3s eccentric) back to neutral flat-foot. Don't bounce or drop — the eccentric is where strength gets built.

sections.label: Cues
sections.content: Top hold is the point of the exercise. It teaches the calf to hold peak contraction and reinforces end-range plantarflexion. Don't sacrifice range for weight — height of rise matters more than pounds on the bar in early weeks.

sections.label: Why flat, not on a step
sections.content: Calf raises on a step lower the heel below the platform — that's additional dorsiflexion under load, which for your pattern adds range you don't need. Flat ground delivers the strengthening benefit without the dorsiflexion emphasis.

sections.label: Progression
sections.content: Weeks 1–2: bodyweight or light dumbbells, focus on height + top hold. Weeks 3–6: progressively add weight. Week 6+: graduate to single-leg eccentric version (below) for the main sets.

sections.label: Setup
sections.content: Stand next to a wall or rack for balance. Hold a dumbbell in one hand if adding load. Stand on flat ground, one foot off (or just lifted behind you).

sections.label: Execution
sections.content: Push up onto the ball of the standing foot using both feet (tap the lifted foot down briefly to assist). Once at the top, lift the assisting foot and lower slowly on just the working leg — 4–5 seconds to the bottom. Tap down, drive up with both, lower with one. Repeat.

sections.label: Cues
sections.content: The eccentric (lowering) phase is the whole point — that's where the strength gap between your two legs or between you and "normal" gets closed. Don't rush it.

sections.label: Progression
sections.content: Once you can do 3 × 6 unloaded with 5s eccentric per rep, start adding weight.

library.title: Banded posterior talar glide with active pointing

sections.label: Setup
sections.content: Anchor a heavy resistance band low on a rack, door anchor, or sturdy post — at floor level or just above. Loop the band around the front of your ankle, just above where the foot meets the shin (so the band pulls on the front of the lower tibia). Sit on the floor facing away from the anchor, band-leg extended, so the band pulls the ankle toward the anchor (which is behind you).

sections.label: Execution
sections.content: With the band loaded, actively point the foot as far as you can, then relax. Repeat 10 times. The band creates a posterior-directed pull on the distal tibia relative to the talus — effectively gliding the talus rearward while you actively reinforce the plantarflexion pattern.

sections.label: Cues
sections.content: Band tension should be firm but not so strong it causes the ankle to hurt. The point is joint mobilization, not compression. If in doubt, go lighter on band tension.

sections.label: Note
sections.content: This is the most technique-dependent exercise in the plan. If it doesn't feel right after a few tries, skip it — the rest of the plan will still work. A PT visit to dial in the technique once is worth the money if you want to use this long-term.

sections.label: Setup
sections.content: From quadruped, bring one knee forward and place the shin at a diagonal under your torso. If flexibility permits, the front shin approaches perpendicular to the body; if not, keep it at a shallower angle. Extend the back leg straight behind you, hip pointing down.

sections.label: Execution
sections.content: Square both hips forward as much as possible. Walk hands out in front and lower chest toward the front leg, either resting on forearms or lying flat.

sections.label: Cues
sections.content: If the front hip is lifting off the floor, place a folded blanket or cushion under it to level off — chasing a perpendicular shin with an elevated hip kills the stretch. Start shallow and work deeper over weeks.

sections.label: Common mistake
sections.content: Forcing the front shin to 90° on day one. Pigeon meets you where you are.

sections.label: Setup
sections.content: Lie on your left side, legs stacked, head resting on left arm.

sections.label: Execution
sections.content: Bend the right knee back, grab the right ankle with your right hand, and pull the heel toward your glute. For more stretch, squeeze the right glute and drive the right knee slightly back (hip extension) while keeping hold of the ankle.

sections.label: Cues
sections.content: Engage your core — don't let the lower back arch. Hips stay stacked vertically; don't let the top hip roll forward or backward.

sections.label: Setup
sections.content: Quadruped.

sections.label: Execution
sections.content: Reach your right arm under your body, threading it toward the left side. Let your right shoulder and the right side of your head come to rest on the floor. Left arm can stay planted, or extend overhead for a deeper lat stretch.

sections.label: Cues
sections.content: Keep hips stacked over your knees — don't let them shift. The rotation should come from the thoracic spine, not the hips or lumbar.

sections.label: Setup
sections.content: Lie on your back with a foam roller placed horizontally across your upper back — roughly between the shoulder blades. Knees bent, feet flat on the floor.

sections.label: Execution
sections.content: Support your head with both hands, elbows pointing up toward the ceiling. Gently lower the head and shoulders back, letting the upper back arch over the roller. Lift back up. Move the roller progressively up and down your thoracic spine — but never below the bottom of the ribcage.

sections.label: Cues
sections.content: Small movements. This is a gentle, accumulated stretch — not a hyperextension. Supporting the head prevents neck strain.

sections.label: Warning
sections.content: Never let the foam roller sit on your lumbar spine. Keep it above the bottom of the ribs.

sections.label: Setup
sections.content: Sit on the floor. Front leg bent 90° in front of you — the shin runs roughly parallel to your pelvis. Back leg bent 90° to the side — shin perpendicular to the front shin. Both knees are bent at 90°, hence the name.

sections.label: Execution
sections.content: Sit tall first. Then hinge forward over the front shin — this stretches the front hip's external rotators (glute/piriformis area). Return upright. Lean back and toward the back leg — this stretches the back hip's internal rotators.

sections.label: Cues
sections.content: Both sit-bones should stay on the floor ideally. If the front-leg-side sit-bone lifts, place a cushion or folded blanket under it to level your pelvis. Keep the 90° angles — don't let the front shin drift closer to your body to "cheat" the stretch.

sections.label: Setup
sections.content: Start seated in the 90/90 position (see above).

sections.label: Execution
sections.content: Sweep both legs up and over to the opposite side, rotating through the hips, and land in a mirrored 90/90 on the other side. Continue alternating.

sections.label: Cues
sections.content: Hands support on the floor behind or beside you as needed. Move slowly with control. This builds active range — the ability to use the range, not just hold it passively.

progressionRules.title: Weeks 1–2 · Prove consistency
progressionRules.text: Cut all hold times in half. Don't chase depth. The single goal is to show up every evening and tolerate the movements without soreness bleeding into your morning lifts.

progressionRules.title: Weeks 3–6 · Full protocol
progressionRules.text: Full hold times. Work into "working stretch" intensity — a clear sensation in the target tissue that's uncomfortable but not sharp, not in a joint. Never push into pain.

progressionRules.title: Week 6+ · Add load and active work
progressionRules.text: Heavier Jefferson curls. Deeper Cossacks. Introduce PNF/contract-relax on hamstrings and adductors: stretch to end range, contract the stretched muscle against resistance for 5–10s, relax, move deeper. This is where real, durable range gains happen.

progressionRules.title: On plateaus
progressionRules.text: If a benchmark stalls for 3+ weeks, add load rather than duration. Holding passive stretches longer has diminishing returns; loaded stretching (Jefferson curl, Cossack with weight, deficit deadlifts) tends to unlock stuck ranges.

progressionRules.title: Timing relative to bed
progressionRules.text: Do the session 30–90 minutes before bed. Immediately before can be too activating (or, paradoxically, make falling asleep harder). The parasympathetic shift from mobility work is real — give it a small window to land.

benchmarks.desc: Seated, leg extended, actively point foot maximally. Measure angle between shin and sole with a goniometer or phone protractor app. Normal is 50°+; this is the key ROM you're chasing.
Can you hold a maximal active toe point for 10s without cramping or losing shape? Start with whatever you can do and extend.
Longest continuous hold at the bottom of a bodyweight squat, heels flat, without support.
Classic test. Seated, legs straight, reach forward. Measure distance past (or short of) your toes.
Chest-to-shin distance in the 90/90 forward lean. Note any sit-bone lift.
Can you hold posterior pelvic tilt for 90s with torso upright? Both sides.
Can you reach parallel (hip crease below knee) on each side with heel down?

ankleNote.title: Built around a specific ankle ROM pattern
paragraphs: Total ankle range is roughly normal, but distributed asymmetrically: abundant dorsiflexion, limited plantarflexion. Likely mechanism — anteriorly-shifted talus, lengthened anterior capsule, shortened posterior capsule, underused calves. The window of motion has slid forward. The goal is to redistribute it rearward.
items: No calf stretching or dorsiflexion-biased ankle work. Standard tools — knee-to-wall, downward dog calf stretch, runner's stretch — are excluded. They deepen range that's already there.
Anterior ankle lengthening. Kneeling on tops of feet and seated shin stretches open the front of the ankle (tibialis anterior, toe extensors, anterior capsule).
Loaded plantarflexion through full range. Weighted calf raises with end-range pauses build calf strength and help seat the talus further back. Post-leg days only (Mon, Thu) to protect squat sessions.
Active plantarflexion drills. Hard toe points and ankle CARs train neural drive into the end range that's currently under-reached.
Optional: banded posterior talar glide. Joint mobilization that pulls the talus rearward during active pointing.
Kept despite dorsiflexion load: deep squat hold and Cossack squat. These serve hip/adductor goals — the ankle position is incidental, not a stretching target.
